lesson.menu_title: Buying Time -- Asking for More Time and Negotiating the Date
lesson.menu_desc: Ask for an extension with two options and a commitment -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Buying Time -- Asking for More Time and Negotiating the Date
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 10 -- Buying Time

plan_row.tema: Buying Time -- Asking for More Time and Negotiating the Date
plan_row.foco: Polite requests (could / would it be possible) + first conditional (the trade)
plan_row.atividade: Extension request with two options + stakeholder who says no
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min extension request with two options

reading.rtitle: The Only Way to Buy Time
paras: Sooner or later, every project manager has to ask for more time. Most of them ask badly. They say 'I need two more weeks', and they hand their stakeholder a problem with no solution. The answer is almost always no.

Strong managers never ask. They trade. They arrive with a reason, two options, and a clear trade-off: the same date with less scope, or the full scope with a new date. The stakeholder is not asked to solve a problem -- only to choose between two answers, and people like choosing.
One more thing separates a professional from an amateur: the commitment. After the trade, say the date out loud and own it. A date you commit to is worth more than a date you hope for.

choices: You should never tell a stakeholder that a deadline is at risk.
Do not ask for time: offer a trade with two options, then commit to a date.
The best way to get more time is to explain the problem in detail.

items: A stakeholder likes to receive a problem with no options.
They want to choose between two answers, not solve your problem.
A good ask contains a reason, two options and a trade-off.
Same date with less scope, or full scope with a new date.
After the trade, you should commit to the date out loud.
A date you commit to is worth more than a date you hope for.

bank.label: How to ask for time
items: The reason is simple: the team does not have the bandwidth.
Could we move the go-live to July 5th?
If we cut the reports, we can keep June 20th.
The trade-off is time for scope.
I can commit to July 5th, with a three-day buffer.

items.situation: Your stakeholder says: 'Just work weekends.' Push back.
tips: Offer the trade instead of refusing.

items.situation: Ask for two more weeks in one sentence -- politely.
tips: Give the new date, not a vague 'more time'.

items.situation: Offer the trade: the same date, but something has to go.
She asks: 'What do I tell the client?'
tips: Give her the two options, in her words.
Say what the client does NOT lose.

items.situation: She gives you one week instead of two. React.
tips: Accept the week, then name the new trade-off.
Do not say yes to something impossible.
Commit to the date out loud.
Promise one document, with a deadline.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Why did you not tell me three weeks ago that June was impossible?
If I give you the two weeks, what happens if you miss July 5th too?
The client will ask for a discount. What do I say?

pressure.text: Answer first, reason second, offer third. Never leave the stakeholder without a choice.

listenings.text: Diogo, I know what you are going to ask me, and I will save you some time. Do not come to me and say that you need more time. That is a problem, and it is not my problem. Come with two options. Option one: a new date that is realistic, and that you can commit to. Option two: the same date, with less scope, and tell me exactly what we cut. Then I can choose, and I can defend the choice with the client. The client will accept a trade. The client will never accept a surprise. And Diogo, one more thing. Whatever we choose, I need a buffer before the go-live.

Thank you for the ten minutes. I will be direct. June 20th is not realistic anymore. The reason is simple: the team does not have the bandwidth for the three custom reports. So I have two options for you. Option A: we keep the full scope, and we move the go-live to July 5th. Option B: we keep June 20th, and we cut the three custom reports, which we deliver in August. In both cases I want a three-day buffer before the go-live. The trade-off is time for scope, and it is your call. Whichever option you choose, I can commit to it, and I will send you the new plan the same day. I need the decision by Friday.

extra_audio.text: Diogo says that June 20th is not realistic anymore. He presents two options: a new date, or the same date with less scope. Sophia refuses two weeks and offers one. Diogo accepts one week and pushes back on the reports. Finally, he commits to June 27th with a three-day buffer.
